title: Class Members
code: oopr("MemberClass",, { public:x <- 1L; }) oopr("ClassMemExample",, { public:   mem <- MemberClass; }) obj <- ClassMemExample(); print(obj); #> <ClassMemExample: 0x556caf476ee8> #>  └─$mem:<MemberClass: 0x556caf473630> #>          └─$x: int 1

title: Class Members
text: Members can become containers classes using syntactic sugar. Suffixing [] uses OoprVec() [[]] uses OoprMap().
code: oopr("ClassMemExample",, { ClassMemExample <- \( ) {   for(i in 1:3)   {     this$mem$emplace();     this$mem[i]$x <- i;   } } public:   mem <- MemberClass[]; # <- use of [] }) obj <- ClassMemExample(); print(obj); #> <ClassMemExample: 0x556cb01a3dd0> #>  └─$mem:<OoprVec: 0x556cb0171870> #>          ├─$class  : chr "MemberClass" #>          ├─$empty  : logi FALSE #>          ├─$size   : int 3 #>          ├─$data   :List of 3 #>          │           $:<MemberClass: 0x556cb0177480> #>          │            ..└─$x: int 1 #>          │           $:<MemberClass: 0x556cb018ab98> #>          │            ..└─$x: int 2 #>          │           $:<MemberClass: 0x556cb019a818> #>          │            ..└─$x: int 3 #>          ├─$insert :\(pos = this$size, x)   #>          ├─$emplace:\(. = this$size)   #>          ├─$resize :\(n)   #>          ├─$erase  :\(pos = this$size)   #>          ├─$swap   :\(pos1, pos2)   #>          ├─$apply  :\(fun, ...)   #>          ├─$[      :\(i, j, ..., drop)   #>          └─$[<-    :\(i, j, ..., value)

title: Class Members
text: class member constructor method, member need : initialized within constructor method, arguments provided member defined. member specified static:, 2. option.
code: oopr("MemberClass",,  {  MemberClass <- \(x) { this$x <- x; } public:x    <- 1L;  })  # not allowed, needs constructing oopr("ClassMemExample",, { public:   mem <- MemberClass; }) #>   Class `mem` must be initialized in the constructor method via #>   `this$mem(...)`. #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors # both approaches oopr("ClassMemExample",, { ClassMemExample <- \( ) {   this$mem1(1L);           # <- args given in constructor } public:   mem1 <- MemberClass;   mem2 <- MemberClass(2L); # <- args given in-line }) obj <- ClassMemExample(); print(obj); #> <ClassMemExample: 0x556cae532700> #>  ├─$mem1:<MemberClass: 0x556cae532c08> #>  │        └─$x: int 1 #>  └─$mem2:<MemberClass: 0x556cae530ea8> #>           └─$x: int 2

title: Inheritance
text: second argument oopr() used inheritance. expects another class via symbol. Multiple inheritance available using brace {.
code: # create some classes oopr("Base1",, { public:shout   <- \( ) print("HELLO!"); }) oopr("Base2",, { public:whisper <- \( ) print("world");  }) # single inheritance oopr("InheritanceExample", Base1, { public:   speak <- \( ) { this$shout(); } }) obj <- InheritanceExample(); obj$speak(); #> [1] "HELLO!" # multiple inheritance oopr("InheritanceExample", { Base1; Base2; }, { public:   speak <- \( )    {      this$shout();      this$whisper();    } }) obj <- InheritanceExample(); obj$speak(); #> [1] "HELLO!" #> [1] "world"

title: Inheritance
text: access base classes can amended using public:, protected:, private: specifiers, influences access base classes members. default, base classes inherited privately. public:, base S3 class included derived instance S3 class list. interaction member access class inheritance access : Inheritance Access Interactions implications: Public inheritance allows members derived class copy base class. Protected members can accessed derived class, private members . Inheriting class privately allow use members derived class, hide derived classes.
code: # Inherits private by default print(obj) #> <InheritanceExample: 0x5559b2c6b568> #>  └─$speak:\()  # Inherit as public oopr("InheritanceExample", public:Base1, { public:   speak <- \( ) { this$shout(); } }) obj <- InheritanceExample();  # Base public members are visible print(obj); #> <InheritanceExample: 0x5559ae6bf8f0> #>  ├─$speak:\()   #>  └─$shout:\()  # it also carries the S3 class print(class(obj)); #> [1] "InheritanceExample" "Base1"              "oopr"

title: Inheritance
text: derived class may create implementation base class method. base class implementation can still used inside derived class methods via base$ instead $:
code: # base class with a method oopr("Base",, { public:   method <- \( ) { print("Base"); } }) # derived class, which redefines the method oopr("Derived", public:Base, { public:   method <- \( )    {      Base$method();     print("Method");    } }) # only one method obj <- Derived(); print(obj); #> <Derived: 0x5559b062dd08> #>  └─$method:\()  # which calls the base class obj$method(); #> [1] "Base" #> [1] "Method"

title: Inheritance
text: final: specifier available stop derived classes redefining methods:
code: oopr("Base",, { public:   final:method <- \( ) { print("Base"); } #<- note final: }) oopr("Derived", public:Base, { public:   method <- \( ) { print("Method"); } }) #>   Method `method` is overridding inherited class `Base` final method. #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors

title: Inheritance
text: base class may allow derived classes implement versions method, require function signature1, useful looping multiple instances different classes share base class. Virtual methods overridden derived class choose , consider form “reverse inheritance”. Although popular languages, virtual methods oopr specified private.
code: # base class with virtual method oopr("Base",, { public:   virtual:method <- \(x, y = 1L) { } }) # does not match arguments oopr("Derived", public:Base,  { public:   method <- \( ) { } }) #>   Method `method` signature () does not match inherited class `Base` #>   virtual method signature (x = , y = 1L): "Not the same amount of #>   arguments". #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors # does not have a default argument oopr("Derived", public:Base,  { public:   method <- \(x = 1L, y) { } }) #>   Method `method` signature (x = 1L, y = ) does not match inherited #>   class `Base` virtual method signature (x = , y = 1L): "Argument "y" #>   must have a default value". #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors # method that relies on a virtual method oopr("Base",, { public:   speak  <- \( ) { this$method(); } protected:   virtual:method <- \( ) { print("Base"); } }) # overridden for the derived class oopr("Derived", public:Base, { protected:   method <- \( ) { print("Derived"); } }) obj <- Derived(); obj$speak() #> [1] "Derived"

title: Inheritance
text: base class constructor method, must explicitly initialized derived class2. initialize, simply call base class constructor within derived customer method: Note base class members accessed constructed:
code: # base class with constructor method oopr("Base",, { Base <- \(x) {   this$x <- x; } public:   x <- integer(1L); })  # unable to create the derived class oopr("Derived", Base, {}) #>   Class `Base` must be initialized in the constructor method via #>   `Base(...)`. #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors # call Base(...) oopr("Derived", public:Base,  { Derived <- \(x) {   Base(x); } })  # success obj <- Derived(1L); print(obj); #> <Derived: 0x5559adec3450> #>  └─$x: int 1 # cannot access `x` before Base(...) oopr("Derived", public:Base,  { Derived <- \(x) {   print(Base$x); # <- neither Base$   print(this$x); # <- nor this$   Base(x); } }) #>   Constructor method `Derived` is using an inherited member `Base$x` #>   prior to initializing the inherited class `Base`. #>  #>   Constructor method `Derived` is using an inherited member `this$x` #>   prior to initializing the inherited class `Base`. #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors

title: Methods
text: running oopr() function, body methods traversed ensure members used exist correctly used. designed reduce bugs introduced typos changing code-bases, aren’t caught creating instances. Many stops employed maintain assumptions oopr classes.
code: oopr("Bad",, { public:   get:prop <- \( ) { return(1L); }   method   <- \( )   {     this$X;          # this does not exist     this$prop();     # this is not a method     this$method(1L); # this does not have any arguments     this$prop <- 1L; # this property has no setter   } }) #>   Member `method` is attempting to refer to an undefined member #>   `this$X`. #>  #>   Member `method` is attempting to call property `this$prop()`. #>  #>   Member `method` call to method `this$method(1L)` does not match its #>   signature: "unused argument (1)". #>  #>   Member `method` is attempting to assign into get property #>   `this$prop`. #> Error in `oopr()`: #> ! Compilation errors

title: Methods
text: Inside methods, allows instance refer . environment S3 class makes faster access. contains members regardless access specifier. object returned constructing instance different environment, S3 classes containing public members. can accessed via .. body method traversed calls return() return(invisible()). found, replaced .. developer wishes return instance avoid using return, use .instead. Using return(invisible()) allows method chaining:
code: oopr("Sentence",,  { public:   x      <- character(1L);   print  <- \( )    {      cat(trimws(this$x), "\n");      return(invisible(this));    }   append <- \(word)    {      this$x <- sprintf("%s %s", this$x, trimws(word));      return(invisible(this));    } }) obj <- Sentence(); obj$append("hello")$print()$append("world")$print(); #> hello  #> hello world

title: Methods
text: Constructor methods can used perform actions constructing instance class. example, allowing user provide initial values members, opening connections elsewhere. define constructor method, define method name class. Constructor methods can private, re-used instance created.
code: oopr("ConstructorExample",, { ConstructorExample <- \(x)  {   cat(sprintf("Constructing with x = %s\n", deparse1(x))); } }) ConstructorExample(2L); #> Constructing with x = 2L #> <ConstructorExample: 0x561a1c8673f0>

title: Methods
text: Destructor methods run action instance destroyed. useful tidying externalities created class. R, object destroyed reached garbage collector runs, different languages. define destructor method, define method constructor method, prefixed ~. destructor method arguments.
code: oopr("DestructorExample",, { ~DestructorExample <- \( ) {   print("Destructing!"); } }) obj <- DestructorExample(); # do work rm(obj); invisible(gc()); #> [1] "Destructing!"

title: Properties
text: Properties can set using get: set: specifiers.
code: oopr("PropertyExample",, { public:   get:x <- \( ) { return(this$x_); }   set:x <- \(x) { this$x_ <- x; } private:   x_ <- integer(1L); }) obj <- PropertyExample();  # calls set obj$x <- 1L;  # calls get print(obj$x); #> [1] 1

title: Properties
text: Members get: specifier must zero argument function. Members set: specifier must one argument function default value. Must defined sequentially. Must access specifier. Must static: least one static:.

title: Properties
text: Properties useful encapsulation fields. Use-cases include:

title: Properties
text: field serve information passed user class, without allowing user edit .
code: oopr("ReadOnly",, { public:   get:x <- \( ) { return(this$x_); } private:   x_ <- integer(1L); }) obj <- ReadOnly();  # can read print(obj$x) #> [1] 0  # but not write obj$x <- 1L; #> Error: #> ! `x` is read-only

title: Properties
text: R type-safe, member class can set arbitrary object user. potential errors methods assume specific type, conducting arithmetic numeric field. Using property setter can assert incoming values ensure meet expectations member. numeric single length vector.
code: oopr("DataValidation",, { public:   get:x <- \( ) { return(this$x_); }   set:x <- \(x)   {     if(!(is.integer(x) && length(x) == 1L))     {       stop("`x` must be a single integer!", call. = FALSE);     }     this$x_ <- x;     return(x);   } private:   x_ <- integer(1L); }) obj <- DataValidation();  # cannot write with unwanted objects obj$x <- 1:2; #> Error: #> ! `x` must be a single integer!

text: Properties like hybrid fields methods, preferred?

text: fields accessed /assigned users always properties data can asserted entering class. Fields classes internal package optional, author understand requirements.

title: Properties
text: setting field requires one argument (incoming value), use method.

title: Properties
text: get property uses lots resources, use method instead. Operations inspect instance (printing) likely call getter function.

title: Properties
text: setting field results side-effects, better implemented method. way name method can describe action.

title: Static Members
text: Members can declared static static: specifier.
code: oopr("StaticExample",, { public:   static:field  <- 1L; }) obj <- StaticExample();  # can be used from the class print(StaticExample); #> <StaticExample ooprC: 0x55f474f496b0> #> Usage: #>   StaticExample()  #> Static Members: #>  └─$field: int 1 StaticExample$field <- 2L;  # instances share the same state with the class print(obj); #> <StaticExample: 0x55f475f8d950> #>  └─$field: int 2

title: Static Members
text: Static methods may refer static members via $. Static methods specified virtual:.

title: Static Members
text: can useful multiple instances share variable:
code: oopr("GlobalStaticExample",, { public:   static:tally <- 0L;   method <- \( ) { this$tally <- this$tally + 1L; } }) obj1 <- GlobalStaticExample(); obj2 <- GlobalStaticExample();  obj1$method(); print(obj2$tally); #> [1] 1  obj2$method(); print(obj1$tally); #> [1] 2

title: Static Members
text: Grouping common functions together single class can keep namespace clean.
code: oopr("UtilsExample",, { public:   static:add      <- \(x, y) { return(x + y); }   static:multiply <- \(x, y) { return(x * y); }   static:subtract <- \(x, y) { return(x - y); } }) UtilsExample$add(1, 1); #> [1] 2 UtilsExample$multiply(1, 1); #> [1] 1 UtilsExample$subtract(1, 1); #> [1] 0

title: Get Started with oopr
text: package uses terminology used Object Oriented Programming (OOP): Class data type, similar list environment R. class like blueprint, defines contents name type. Member contents class members. member named can either hold data (field), conduct action (method). Field Data members hold objects. example number string. Method Members conduct action. functions within class. Instance instance object follows blueprint class. instance holds members defined class, work independently -.

title: Get Started with oopr
text: get started, simple example demonstrated . -depth discussions functionality package can found relevant vignettes.

title: Get Started with oopr
text: Lets create class represents counter. counter hold number record many times counted, method increment number: things note : name class given string first argument. second argument blank (reserved inheritance). definition class within { ... } third argument. Within definition two members defined: x: field representing integer. incr(): method increases x 1L. use inside method allows object class refer members. case, incr() can access x via $x. use public: allows user class access members, later.
code: oopr("Counter",, { public:   x    <- 0L;   incr <- \( ) { this$x <- this$x + 1L; } }) print(Counter) #> <Counter ooprC: 0x562e5ea6da68> #> Usage: #>   Counter()

title: Get Started with oopr
text: create instances class, call class object function: access members class instance, use $ operator:
code: # initialize the class for a new instance obj <- Counter(); print(obj); #> <Counter: 0x562e5e73d728> #>  ├─$x   : int 0 #>  └─$incr:\() # use the method obj$incr();  # access the field print(obj$x); #> [1] 1

title: Get Started with oopr
text: class issue: $incr() method assumes $x always integer. user accidentally break assumption: prevent user amending $x outside using $incr(), private: specifier can used. private member can accessed inside class. integer member now named $x_ use private: prevents directly accessed user still allowing $incr() access . Users may need access count public getter method $x() introduced. relies restriction class methods re-defined.
code: # whoops! obj$x <- "a";  # now the instance is broken obj$incr(); #> Error in `this$x + 1L`: #> ! non-numeric argument to binary operator oopr("Counter",, { public:   x    <- \( ) { return(this$x_); }   incr <- \( ) { this$x_ <- this$x_ + 1L; } private:                                   # <- note private: here   x_   <- 0L; }) obj <- Counter();  # can still access x print(obj$x()); #> [1] 0  # but cannot overwrite it obj$x <- "a"; #> Error in `obj$x <- "a"`: #> ! cannot change value of locked binding for 'x'

title: Get Started with oopr
text: quality life improvement ability treat $x member normal field instead needing call method. get: specifier can prepended zero-argument function treat property.
code: oopr("Counter",, { public:   get:x <- \( ) { return(this$x_); }        # <- note get: here   incr  <- \( ) { this$x_ <- this$x_ + 1L; } private:   x_   <- 0L; }) obj <- Counter();  # x looks like a normal field print(obj$x); #> [1] 0  # but it has no setter, making it more robust obj$x <- "a"; #> Error: #> ! `x` is read-only

title: Get Started with oopr
text: multiple specifiers available change members class, explained vignettes. : operator used specify different behaviour class members. sides : must symbols, right-symbol become name member.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Bachetti N (2026). oopr: Classes Object Oriented Programming. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://bochotti.github.io/oopr/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {oopr: Classes for Object Oriented Programming},   author = {Nathan Bachetti},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://bochotti.github.io/oopr/}, }

title: Classes for Object Oriented Programming
text: package created assist writing classes R code. methods::setRefClass() R6::R6Class() already provide functionality, package implements idea differently. Class implementations defined within single brace {. Members can different behavior via use specifiers. Upon creating class, series checks performed reduce risk run-time errors. Code-completion breakpoints supported RStudio. Classes useful bundling data actions together, “group” common functions.

title: Classes for Object Oriented Programming
code: ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## #  human as a class ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## oopr("Human",, { ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## Human <- \(first, last, age) {   stopifnot(     this$isScalar("character", first)    ,this$isScalar("character", last)    ,this$isScalar("integer"  , age)   );   this$first_ <- first;   this$last_  <- last;   this$age_   <- age; } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## public:   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   get:name <- \( )   {     return(sprintf(       "%s %s", this$first_, this$last_     ));   }   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   greet <- \( )   {     cat(sprintf(       "Hello, my name is %s, aged %i\n"      ,this$name, this$age_     ));     return(invisible(this));   } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## private:   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   first_ <- character(1L);   last_  <- character(1L);   age_   <- integer(0L);   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   isScalar <- \(type, x)   {     return(          length(x) == 1L       && typeof(x) == type     );   } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## }) ## Human ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##  john <- Human("john", "smith", 50L); print(john); #> <Human: 0x6103bc3ecb58> #>  ├─$name : chr "john smith" #>  └─$greet:\() john$greet(); #> Hello, my name is john smith, aged 50

code: ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## #  human as a class ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## oopr("Human",, { ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## Human <- \(first, last, age) {   stopifnot(     this$isScalar("character", first)    ,this$isScalar("character", last)    ,this$isScalar("integer"  , age)   );   this$first_ <- first;   this$last_  <- last;   this$age_   <- age; } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## public:   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   get:name <- \( )   {     return(sprintf(       "%s %s", this$first_, this$last_     ));   }   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   greet <- \( )   {     cat(sprintf(       "Hello, my name is %s, aged %i\n"      ,this$name, this$age_     ));     return(invisible(this));   } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## private:   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   first_ <- character(1L);   last_  <- character(1L);   age_   <- integer(0L);   ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##   isScalar <- \(type, x)   {     return(          length(x) == 1L       && typeof(x) == type     );   } ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## }) ## Human ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ##  john <- Human("john", "smith", 50L); print(john); #> <Human: 0x55edd2eccf68> #>  ├─$name : chr "john smith" #>  └─$greet:\()   john$greet(); #> Hello, my name is john smith, aged 50

title: Breakpoints for oopr — oopr_breakpoints
text: logical(1L) indicating whether functionality enabled. breakpoints set printed console.

title: Breakpoints for oopr — oopr_breakpoints
text: Setting breakpoints inside oopr classes process setting normal functions - use gutter SHIFT+F9. feature requires intercepting RStudio calls functions normally reside within tools:rstudio, assigning functions name inside global environment. disabled default potentially destructive R session. Environmental variable R_OOPR_BREAKPOINTS=true can used enable functionality oopr namespace loaded. Breakpoints can set methods properties. One limitation breakpoints set single line functions specifier,   RStudio thinks line top-level. breakpoint set class instances searched global environment package namespace (applicable). function inside instance match source file, breakpoints removed function instance. Tested RStudio version 2026.1.1.403, guarantees earlier later versions.

title: Containers for oopr Instances — oopr_containers
code: ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## oopr("test",, { test <- \(x) { this$x <- x; } public:x <- 0L; }) ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## # create a vector vec <- OoprVec(test);  # $emplace create a new instance vec$emplace(, 1L);  # $insert passes existing instance vec$insert(0L, test(2L));  print(vec); #> <OoprVec: 0x55edd7bde070> #>  ├─$class  : chr "test" #>  ├─$empty  : logi FALSE #>  ├─$size   : int 2 #>  ├─$data   :List of 2 #>  │           $:<test: 0x55edd7fcffd0> #>  │            ..└─$x: int 2 #>  │           $:<test: 0x55edd7f8b178> #>  │            ..└─$x: int 1 #>  ├─$insert :\(pos = this$size, x)   #>  ├─$emplace:\(. = this$size, x)   #>  ├─$resize :\(n)   #>  ├─$erase  :\(pos = this$size)   #>  ├─$swap   :\(pos1, pos2)   #>  ├─$apply  :\(fun, ...)   #>  ├─$[      :\(i, j, ..., drop)   #>  └─$[<-    :\(i, j, ..., value)    # $swap will swap elements vec$swap(2L, 1L); print(vec); #> <OoprVec: 0x55edd7bde070> #>  ├─$class  : chr "test" #>  ├─$empty  : logi FALSE #>  ├─$size   : int 2 #>  ├─$data   :List of 2 #>  │           $:<test: 0x55edd7f8b178> #>  │            ..└─$x: int 1 #>  │           $:<test: 0x55edd7fcffd0> #>  │            ..└─$x: int 2 #>  ├─$insert :\(pos = this$size, x)   #>  ├─$emplace:\(. = this$size, x)   #>  ├─$resize :\(n)   #>  ├─$erase  :\(pos = this$size)   #>  ├─$swap   :\(pos1, pos2)   #>  ├─$apply  :\(fun, ...)   #>  ├─$[      :\(i, j, ..., drop)   #>  └─$[<-    :\(i, j, ..., value)    # $apply to loop over instances vec$apply(\(x) { x$x <- x$x + 1L; }) #> [[1]] #> [1] 2 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 3 #>  vec$apply(\(x) { x$x; }) #> [[1]] #> [1] 2 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 3 #>  ## ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ## # create a key-value pair map <- OoprMap(test); map$emplace("a", "a")$emplace("b", "b"); print(map); #> <OoprMap: 0x55edd887fe40> #>  ├─$class  : chr "test" #>  ├─$empty  : logi FALSE #>  ├─$size   : int 2 #>  ├─$keys   : chr [1:2] "a" "b" #>  ├─$data   :List of 2 #>  │           $a:<test: 0x55edd88ab750> #>  │            ..└─$x: chr "a" #>  │           $b:<test: 0x55edd88aebf0> #>  │            ..└─$x: chr "b" #>  ├─$exists :\(key)   #>  ├─$insert :\(key, x)   #>  ├─$emplace:\(., x)   #>  ├─$erase  :\(key)   #>  ├─$resize :\(keys = character(0L))   #>  ├─$apply  :\(fun, ...)   #>  ├─$[      :\(i, j, ..., drop)   #>  └─$[<-    :\(i, j, ..., value)    # apply is a two argument function map$apply(\(k, o) { o$x <- toupper(k); }); #> $a #> [1] "A" #>  #> $b #> [1] "B" #>  map$apply(\(k, o) { o$x == k; }); #> $a #> [1] FALSE #>  #> $b #> [1] FALSE #>

title: Load oopr in Packages — oopr_onInstall
text: Active bindings preserved package installation (see bindenv), threatening functionality oopr classes. Proposed solution serialise ooprC objects within package namespace installation, unserialise upon package loading. ooprC objects serialised together oopr_onInstall maintain references (see serialize). However, defining environments outside classes lose reference. Inherited classes class members different package taken respective originating namespace oopr_onLoad. TODO: better convert active bindings back functions save location install, convert back active bindings onLoad?
